title: Empirical pit-window hazard priors
summary: Pit timing is naturally a hazard problem: probability of stopping rises with tyre age, compound, race progress, and stint number.
rationale: Pit timing is naturally a hazard problem: probability of stopping rises with tyre age, compound, race progress, and stint number. Strategy models often optimize pit epochs and use tyre degradation curves, and a simple hazard prior can complement GBDTs without requiring a full survival model.
implementation_hint: From training only, compute smoothed historical PitNextLap rates by bins such as Race x Compound x Stint x TyreLifeBin, Race x RaceProgressBin x Stint, Driver x Compound x TyreLifeBin. Add these as OOF target-encoded features using race/year folds or K-fold smoothing; for test, fit encoders on all train with global fallback.
expected_effect: Can add high-signal calibrated priors for common pit windows that tree models may not infer robustly from sparse categorical combinations.
risk: Naive target encoding over all train will inflate CV; must be OOF and smoothed to avoid leakage and overfitting rare race/driver cells.
disabled_reason: Disabled because an external duplicate-detection mechanism marked this hypothesis as duplicate of an existing hypothesis.
